fr:mainmatter.fr:p:
De Bruijn levels are a way of representing variables in a term without using names. Instead of using indices, we use levels to indicate how many binders away the variable is from the root of the term, making levels absolute. In this representation, the variable x is represented by the level 0, the variable y is represented by the level 1 and the variable z is represented by the level 2. This gives us the following level representations of these closed terms:
\begin {align*} \lambda \overset {\red {0}}{\vphantom {y}x}. \lambda \overset {\gray {1}}{y}. \lambda \overset {\gray {2}}{\vphantom {y}z}. \underset {\red {0}}{x} &\equiv \lambda \lambda \lambda \red {0} \\ \lambda \overset {\gray {0}}{\vphantom {y}x}. \lambda \overset {\red {1}}{y}. \lambda \overset {\gray {2}}{\vphantom {y}z}. \underset {\gray {1}}{y} &\equiv \lambda \lambda \lambda \red {1} \\ \lambda \overset {\gray {0}}{\vphantom {y}x}. \lambda \overset {\gray {1}}{y}. \lambda \overset {\red {2}}{\vphantom {y}z}. \underset {\red {2}}{z} &\equiv \lambda \lambda \lambda red{2} \end {align*}

fr:title.text: De Bruijn indices versus de Bruijn levels

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: De Bruijn indices versus de Bruijn levels

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: De Bruijn indices are a way of representing variables in a term without using names. Instead of naming variables, we use numbers to indicate how many binders away the variable is from its binding site. This makes indices relative to the binding site.

De Bruijn levels are a way of representing variables in a term without using names. Instead of using indices, we use levels to indicate how many binders away the variable is from the root of the term, making levels absolute. In this representation, the variable x is represented by the level 0, the variable y is represented by the level 1 and the variable z is represented by the level 2. This gives us the following level representations of these closed terms:
\begin {align*} \lambda \overset {\red {0}}{\vphantom {y}x}. \lambda \overset {\gray {1}}{y}. \lambda \overset {\gray {2}}{\vphantom {y}z}. \underset {\red {0}}{x} &\equiv \lambda \lambda \lambda \red {0} \\ \lambda \overset {\gray {0}}{\vphantom {y}x}. \lambda \overset {\red {1}}{y}. \lambda \overset {\gray {2}}{\vphantom {y}z}. \underset {\gray {1}}{y} &\equiv \lambda \lambda \lambda \red {1} \\ \lambda \overset {\gray {0}}{\vphantom {y}x}. \lambda \overset {\gray {1}}{y}. \lambda \overset {\red {2}}{\vphantom {y}z}. \underset {\red {2}}{z} &\equiv \lambda \lambda \lambda red{2} \end {align*}

On the other hand, levels are better suited for keeping free variables stable in open terms. Consider the following example containing the free variable g and using indices (where \Gamma _s is the context stack):
\begin {aligned} & \Gamma _s = [\overset {\red {3}}{g}, \overset {\red {2}}{x}, \overset {\gray {1}}{y}, \overset {\gray {0}}{z}] & \\ & \lambda \overset {\red {2}}{x}. \lambda \overset {\gray {1}}{y}. \lambda \overset {\gray {0}}{z}. \underset {\red {3}}{g}~\underset {\red {2}}{\vphantom {g}x}~ \lambda \overset {\red {0}}{x}. \underset {\red {0}}{x} &\equiv & \lambda \lambda \lambda \red {3}~\red {2}~ \lambda \red {0} \\ \hline & \Gamma _s = [\overset {\red {3}}{g}, \overset {\gray {1}}{y}, \overset {\gray {0}}{z}] & \\ \rightarrow _\beta ~ & \lambda \overset {\gray {1}}{y}. \lambda \overset {\gray {0}}{z}. \underset {\red {3}}{g}~ \lambda \overset {\red {0}}{\vphantom {g}x}. \underset {\red {0}}{\vphantom {g}x} &\equiv & \lambda \lambda \lambda \red {3}~\red {0} \left .\right \rbrace \hbox {\footnotesize \text {bad free index}}\\ \hline & \Gamma _s = [\overset {\red {2}}{g}, \overset {\gray {1}}{y}, \overset {\gray {0}}{z}] & \\ \rightarrow ~ & \lambda \overset {\gray {1}}{y}. \lambda \overset {\gray {0}}{z}. \underset {\red {2}}{g}~ \lambda \overset {\red {0}}{\vphantom {g}x}. \underset {\red {0}}{\vphantom {g}x} &\equiv & \lambda \lambda \lambda \red {2}~\red {0} \end {aligned}
Now consider the same example using levels:
\begin {aligned} & \Gamma _s = [\overset {\red {0}}{g}, \overset {\gray {1}}{x}, \overset {\gray {2}}{y}, \overset {\gray {3}}{z}] \\ & \lambda \overset {\red {1}}{x}. \lambda \overset {\gray {2}}{y}. \lambda \overset {\gray {3}}{z}. \underset {\red {0}}{g}~\underset {\red {1}}{\vphantom {g}x}~ \lambda \overset {\red {1}}{x}. \underset {\red {1}}{x} &\equiv & \lambda \lambda \lambda \red {0}~\red {1}~\lambda \red {1} \\ \hline & \Gamma _s = [\overset {\red {0}}{g}, \overset {\gray {2}}{y}, \overset {\gray {3}}{z}] \\ \rightarrow _\beta ~ & \lambda \overset {\gray {2}}{y}. \lambda \overset {\gray {3}}{z}. \underset {\red {0}}{g}~\lambda \overset {\red {1}}{x}. \underset {\red {1}}{\vphantom {g}x} &\equiv & \lambda \lambda \lambda \red {0}~\lambda \red {1} \left .\right \rbrace \hbox {\footnotesize \text {bad binding levels}}\\ \hline & \Gamma _s = [\overset {\red {0}}{g}, \overset {\gray {1}}{y}, \overset {\gray {2}}{z}] \\ \rightarrow ~ & \lambda \overset {\gray {1}}{y}. \lambda \overset {\gray {2}}{z}. \underset {\red {0}}{g}~\lambda \overset {\red {3}}{x}. \underset {\red {3}}{\vphantom {g}x} &\equiv & \lambda \lambda \lambda \red {0}~\lambda \red {3} \end {aligned}
Furthemore, levels preserve the identity of bound variables without needing to adjust them. Consider the case using indices and two instances of the same variable x:
\lambda x. (x \lambda y. x y) \equiv \lambda 0~\lambda 1~0
and compare it to the case using levels:
\lambda x. (x~\lambda y. x~y) \equiv \lambda 0~\lambda 0~1

De Bruijn indices are a way of representing variables in a term without using names. Instead of naming variables, we use numbers to indicate how many binders away the variable is from its binding site. This makes indices relative to the binding site.

De Bruijn indices are a way of representing variables in a term without using names. Instead of naming variables, we use numbers to indicate how many binders away the variable is from its binding site. This makes indices relative to the binding site.
